retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Tooth -- Issues of Concern
retrieved_docs.content: Poor dental and oral health can potentially lead to numerous issues. Of particular concern is plaque, a biofilm of various bacteria (mainly Streptococcus and anaerobes) that forms on the surface of teeth. Brushing alone does not always remove plaque in its entirety. If plaque is not professionally removed regularly, periodontal problems and gingivitis can set in. Plaque can mineralize, eventually leading to tartar build-up, causing gingivitis, halitosis, and dental caries.

retrieved_docs.title: Periodontitis -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: Dental plaque is a complex biofilm, which is the colonization of bacteria enclosed by a protective matrix. This matrix is composed of extracellular polysaccharide and glycoproteins providing a protective environment for microbes in the dental biofilm. [18] This component of the dental biofilm makes it 1000 to 1500 times more resistant to antimicrobial agents. [2] The various circulatory channels present in the biofilm aid in the distribution of many nutrients and excretion of the generated metabolic wastes. "Quorum sensing" is a mode of communication between the microcolonies of bacteria within the dental biofilm. [19] "Autoinducers" are molecules secreted by microbes; the concentration of these molecules helps in regulating bacterial gene expression. [19] The biofilm, by different pH concentrations and metabolites, has various microenvironments within the biofilm. These microenvironments make the ecosystem suitable for a variety of microbes inhabiting in the same dental plaque. [18]

retrieved_docs.title: Periodontitis -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: The initial layer deposited on the surface of teeth in the formation of dental plaque is "acquired pellicle." [20] This layer is formed within seconds of the exposure of tooth surfaces, followed by the initial attachment of the early colonizers of the biofilm. Streptococcus and Actinomyces species are the primary colonizers which are gram-positive, facultative bacteria. [20] The "adhesin receptors" present on the surface of primary colonizers bind with the proline-rich proteins of the pellicle. This binding leads to revealing of receptor sites known as "cryptitope" which further leads to coaggregation. [21] Gradually there is a layer by layer deposition of dental plaque leading to a deficiency of oxygen in the environment eventually leading to colonization of anaerobic bacteria. [22] The bridging microbe between primary and secondary colonizers is Fusobacterium species. [22] The gradual shift of these aerobic conditions to anaerobic condition marks the progression of gingivitis to periodontitis. Socransky et al. in a study divided the microbes into microbial complexes based on colors. [23] [5] Red and orange complexes bacteria present in the subgingival area are intricately associated with periodontal disease. [5]

retrieved_docs.title: Halitosis -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: In physiological halitosis, which occurs on waking, the cause is putrefaction of entrapped food particles and desquamated epithelial cells by bacteria. [9] The surface of the tongue has desquamated epithelial cells, leucocytes from periodontal pockets, food residues, and bacteria. The depth of the tongue papillae affects the biofilm coating on top, which prevents the cleansing action of saliva and promotes the growth of anaerobic bacteria, giving rise to halitosis. This occurs even in individuals with healthy periodontal tissues and good dental hygiene. [26] [14] [15] [16] [27] [28]

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Tooth -- Issues of Concern
retrieved_docs.content: Streptococcus mutans is the most important bacterium associated with dental caries. [3] Bacteria live off the remains of food, especially that of sugar and starches. In the absence of oxygen, these bacteria produce lactic acid, dissolving calcium and phosphorus in the enamel. Demineralization of enamel then leads to tooth destruction. Remineralization of enamel occurs through saliva, which keeps the tooth surface above a critical pH of 5.5. Of concern, however, is that saliva is unable to penetrate through plaque, and demineralization can occur. [4]

retrieved_docs.title: Periodontitis -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Approximately 700 species of microorganisms colonize the human oral cavity. [1] These bacteria inhabiting the human oral cavity are mainly commensals along with a sparse population of pathogenic bacteria. [2] Periodontitis is one of the most common ailments affecting the teeth, leading to the destruction of the supporting and surrounding tooth structure. [3] The term "periodontitis" is build up of two words, i.e., "periodont-" meaning "structure surrounding the teeth" and "itis" means "inflammation." Periodontitis is originally a disease originating from the gingival tissue which if left untreated results in penetration of inflammation to the deeper tissues, altering the bone homeostasis causing tooth loss. [3] Periodontal disease has a multifactorial origin. [4] The main culprit identified in periodontitis is the bacterial biofilm growing on the tooth surfaces. [5] [6] While the host response determines the progression of the disease along with factors like local factors like plaque and calculus, genetics, environmental factors, systemic health of the patient, lifestyle habits and various social determinants also play a role. [4] The deleterious effects of periodontopathogens are not limited to the periodontium, but they also exude their ill effects on the systemic health of the patients. [7]

retrieved_docs.title: Apical Periodontitis -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: The now infected and necrotic root canal system is a habitat that allows for the growth and establishment of a mixed, predominately anaerobic flora, which mainly grows in sessile biofilms and aggregates or co-aggregates. [6] The microbial invaders or their by-products can advance into the periapex of the root. In response, the host mounts a defensive mechanism through various cells, intracellular mediators, and humoral antibodies. [7]

retrieved_docs.title: Remineralization of Initial Carious Lesions -- Function -- Pathophysiology of Dental Caries
retrieved_docs.content: Under normal circumstances, the concentration of calcium and phosphate in oral fluids (saliva and biofilm fluid) is supersaturated compared to their concentration in the enamel. These ions are continually deposited on the enamel surface or redeposited on enamel areas where they were previously lost. But when bacteria convert sugars from the diet into acids, the biofilm fluid becomes undersaturated compared to the enamel, leading to demineralization (mineral loss). Even though pH levels remain low (acidic) for a certain time, when the amount of sugar available goes down, the pH of the biofilm fluid returns to neutrality (rises) and becomes sufficiently saturated with calcium, phosphate, and fluoride so that demineralization stops and redeposition of mineral starts. Due to the biofilm calcium and phosphate, redeposition of minerals can occur or by the direct action of salivary calcium and phosphate soon after the biofilm is removed by toothbrushing. [7]

retrieved_docs.title: Gingivitis -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: This is the most common cause of gingivitis. Plaque is a thin film that forms on the tooth surface due to poor oral hygiene. If not regularly removed, it can harden up and form calculus. As plaque harbors a large number of bacteria, inflammation can occur in the gingival tissue. Some local factors can contribute to the formation of plaque, such as crowding of teeth, which makes plaque removal difficult. As misaligned teeth often require orthodontic correction, cleaning difficulty increases, resulting in the accumulation of more plaque. Furthermore, a dental prosthesis that does not have an adequate fit or is not properly finished can also act as a nidus for plaque accumulation. In children, tooth eruption is frequently associated with gingivitis as plaque accumulation tends to increase in the area where primary teeth are exfoliating, and permanent teeth are erupting, as oral hygiene may be difficult to maintain in these areas. This is referred to as eruptive gingivitis.

retrieved_docs.title: Dental Caries -- Pathophysiology -- Microbial Role
retrieved_docs.content: The ecological plaque hypothesis believes that dental caries is not caused by a specific type of microorganism acting alone but is the result of a shift in the microbiota of the dental biofilm towards more cariogenic species. [3] Oral acidic conditions from regular sugar consumption select the bacteria that empathize more with this environment and eliminate the benign species that do not tolerate such conditions. [3]

retrieved_docs.title: Fungal Infections of the Oral Mucosa -- Pathophysiology -- Oral Candidiasis
retrieved_docs.content: Candida albicans is a commensal fungi in the oral cavity. Commensal microorganisms reside in the external surface of the body or mucosa without affecting human health. However, they may become pathological under appropriate conditions, causing superficial to invasive disseminated infections. Candida reversibly attaches to the oral epithelial cells through electrostatic interactions in its commensal yeast state. [4] Cell-wall receptors like the agglutinin-like sequence family of glycoproteins mediate the attachment. [4] The yeasts change their morphology to filamentous hyphal form whenever the host defense mechanism is impaired, known as phenotypic switching, which is critical for the invasion of the host tissue. [4] Extracellular hydrolytic enzymes like secreted aspartyl proteinases and secreted phospholipases produced by C albicans mediate the destruction of host tissue and subsequent invasion. [4] C albicans can form biofilms on denture bases, increasing virulence and decreasing antifungal susceptibility. [33]

retrieved_docs.title: Remineralization of Initial Carious Lesions -- Function -- Pathophysiology of Dental Caries
retrieved_docs.content: All the tooth surfaces are susceptible to caries throughout an individual's lifetime. But, the disease will not occur unless teeth are exposed to cariogenic biofilm and frequent sugar consumption. The modern concept of caries also considers other factors, such as sociodemographic situation, access to dental care, and fluoride exposure, which are significant but not decisive to develop the disease. For all of this, dental caries should be described as a complex biofilm-mediated affection linked mostly to frequent ingestion of sugars and poor oral hygiene combined with inadequate fluoride exposure. [3]

retrieved_docs.title: Dental Caries Diagnostic Testing -- Procedures
retrieved_docs.content: Dental explorers have traditionally diagnosed carious lesions; however, studies have shown that this method causes irreversible harm to the tooth surface and favors the lesion's progression. [3] Dental explorers can disrupt remineralization and transfer cariogenic bacteria to adjacent teeth. Furthermore, using dental explorers to assess occlusal surfaces can result in misdiagnosis - a deep fissure that seems to be a "sticky occlusal surface" could be misdiagnosed as dental caries. Dental explorers should only be used to remove stains and soft plaque from tooth surfaces. [4] Visual examination always comes first when assessing for caries. It requires a dry surface and can be enhanced by magnifying loupes.

retrieved_docs.title: Periodontitis -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: The primary causative agent resulting in periodontal disease is the mixed bacterial colonization in the oral tissue. [2] [8] While there are other factors which act as secondary etiologic factors accelerating the propagation and development of periodontal diseases like developmental grooves, calculus, dental plaque, overhanging restorations, anatomical features like the short trunk, cervical enamel projections, systemic factors, genetic factors, smoking, and stress. [9] [10]

retrieved_docs.title: Dental Caries -- Pathophysiology -- Demineralization and remineralization
retrieved_docs.content: Fermentable carbohydrates are metabolized by the biofilm bacteria that produce organic acids, primarily lactic acid. These end products of bacterial metabolism accumulate in the fluid phase of the biofilm, causing a pH drop and demineralization of the surface layer of the tooth. [12]

retrieved_docs.title: The Role of Fluoride on Caries Prevention -- Function
retrieved_docs.content: As previously mentioned, fluoride incorporated into the enamel crystal structure at the tooth developmental stages does not have a sufficient effect on the acid solubility of enamel. [9] [10] However, the fluoride present in the biofilm due to regular topical exposure surrounds the carbonated apatite crystals (enamel crystals) effectively inhibiting tooth demineralization. At the time of bacterial acid production, the fluoride in the plaque fluid travels down with the acid into the enamel subsurface, adsorbs into its crystal structure, and protects it against dissolution. Fluorapatite Ca 10 (PO 4 ) 6 F 2 is an enamel mineral much more resistant to acid dissolution than other tooth minerals (carbonated apatite and hydroxyapatite). Fluorapatite results from replacing the OH ion in hydroxyapatite with a fluoride ion. [8] Fluoride promotes remineralization

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 1 -- Community 72
retrieved_docs.content: The category “diseases of mouth; excluding dental” is classified as a subcategory of “اِضطِراب الغدة اللُّعابِيَّة” (salivary gland disorder), indicating that non‑dental oral diseases are considered part of the broader spectrum of salivary gland pathology. This relationship highlights the importance of evaluating salivary gland function when assessing oral conditions that are not of dental origin, thereby guiding more comprehensive diagnosis and patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 23 -- Level 2 -- Community 895
retrieved_docs.content: 0.0088 MG/MG Toothpaste. Across these preparations, sodium monofluorophosphate provides fluoride ions that inhibit demineralization and promote remineralization of tooth enamel, thereby reducing the incidence of dental caries.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 1 -- Community 250
retrieved_docs.content: The extraction of a single tooth is a direct dental procedure performed on the zuby (teeth), involving the removal of one tooth from the oral cavity.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1583
retrieved_docs.content: Antiinfectives and antiseptics for local oral treatment are classified as members of the tetracycline group, indicating that these locally applied oral antimicrobial agents belong to the broader tetracycline class of antibiotics and share its characteristic antimicrobial properties.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 1 -- Community 244
retrieved_docs.content: Extraction of single tooth is classified as a dental procedure, indicating that it has a relationship with the broader category of dental procedures. This connection underscores that the act of extracting a single tooth is a specific type of dental intervention within the overall field of dental procedures.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 15 -- Level 3 -- Community 1531
retrieved_docs.content: Antiinfectives and antiseptics for local oral treatment are classified as members of the tetracyklin group, and tetracyklin is identified as a subcategory within the broader class of Tetracycline Antibiotics. This hierarchy indicates that the oral anti‑infective preparations belonging to tetracyklin share the characteristic antimicrobial properties of tetracycline antibiotics, while also being specifically formulated for local use in the mouth. Recognizing that tetracyklin agents are part of the larger Tetracycline Antibiotics family helps clinicians understand their mechanism of action, spectrum of activity, and appropriate therapeutic role in patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 3 -- Community 2732
retrieved_docs.content: Accidental physical contact (event) is described as being due to loss of teeth caused by an accident, indicating that dental injury resulting from an accident can lead to unintended physical contact.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 30 -- Level 1 -- Community 170
retrieved_docs.content: adverse‑event reporting and clinical management. Recognizing sodium monofluorophosphate as a fluoride source guides oral‑health preventive strategies, while awareness of the SMQ classification aids clinicians in systematic identification and treatment of functional GI disturbances, underscoring the importance of these entities for patient care and product design.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 14 -- Level 3 -- Community 1842
retrieved_docs.content: Domiphen is classified as a member of the antiinfectives and antiseptics for local oral treatment category, indicating its role as a topical antimicrobial agent used in oral cavity care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 1 -- Community 131
retrieved_docs.content: 0.0076 mg/mg toothpaste, sodium monofluorophosphate 0.008 mg/mg toothpaste, and sodium monofluorophosphate 0.0088 mg/mg toothpaste. Overall, the knowledge network connects caries‑preventive agents, fluoride‑based supplements, and various oral care preparations through shared classifications (stomatological preparations), chemical constituents (tin fluoride, sodium fluoride, sodium monofluorophosphate), and product‑level active‑ingredient relationships, underscoring the central role of fluoride compounds in dental prophylaxis and therapeutic oral formulations.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 1 -- Community 249
retrieved_docs.content: Dental procedures are performed directly on zuby mléčné (the primary or “milk” teeth), indicating that therapeutic dental interventions often target the primary dentition. Likewise, disorders of teeth and jaw are located in zuby mléčné, showing that many oral pathologies also affect these early‑life teeth. Together, these relationships emphasize that both clinical treatments and disease processes in pediatric dentistry are closely linked to the primary teeth, underscoring the importance of careful assessment and management of zuby mléčné in patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 11 -- Level 1 -- Community 97
retrieved_docs.content: electrolyte solutions, SMQ‑defined disorders), infectious disease (antibiotic granules), nutrition (supplements), and oral health (fluoride dentifrices). Their dual roles as active agents and excipients underscore their broad importance in patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 22 -- Level 3 -- Community 1697
retrieved_docs.content: Antiinfectives and antiseptics for local oral treatment constitute a therapeutic category aimed at managing infections within the mouth, and this category includes active agents such as natamycin and domiphen. Natamycin, an antifungal compound, is incorporated into oral formulations to inhibit the growth of fungal pathogens, while domiphen, a quaternary ammonium antiseptic, contributes antimicrobial activity by disrupting microbial cell membranes. Together, these ingredients provide a combined antifungal and antiseptic effect that enhances the efficacy of locally applied oral treatments for preventing and controlling oral infections.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 7 -- Level 3 -- Community 1459
retrieved_docs.content: Microbiology is identified as an applicable qualifier for the oral conditions قيلة مثانية, قيلة مستقيمية, and اضطراب اللسان, indicating that microbiological factors are relevant in the assessment or management of these disorders. Bacterial vaccines are, in turn, an applicable qualifier for microbiology, reflecting the role of immunization strategies within the broader microbiological context. The term “Without” is noted as not being associated with either قيلة مثانية or قيلة مستقيمية, suggesting that the absence of a particular factor does not have a direct relationship with these specific conditions. Together, these connections highlight the importance of microbiological considerations and vaccine‑related interventions in certain oral pathologies, while clarifying that “Without” does not contribute to their etiology or presentation.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 3 -- Community 1607
retrieved_docs.content: Antiinfectives and antiseptics for local oral treatment represent a therapeutic class of agents used to control infection and maintain hygiene within the mouth. Within this class, hydrogen peroxide (peroxid vodíku) is recognized as a member, providing oxidative antimicrobial activity that helps reduce bacterial load on oral tissues. The quaternary ammonium compound domiphen is also included as a member of this same group, offering a different mechanism of antiseptic action through membrane disruption of oral microorganisms. By classifying both hydrogen peroxide and domiphen as members of the antiinfectives and antiseptics for local oral treatment category, the relationship highlights their shared purpose in oral care despite their distinct chemical properties. This integrated view supports clinicians in selecting appropriate agents from a common therapeutic family to achieve effective local oral infection control.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 17 -- Level 5 -- Community 2933
retrieved_docs.content: Fatigue (التَّعَب) is described as having anxiety (القلق) as a defining characteristic, indicating that patients who experience fatigue often also report significant anxious feelings. Clinically, fatigue is also associated with oral candidiasis (داء المبيضات الفموي), suggesting that the presence of persistent tiredness may coincide with or signal an underlying yeast infection of the mouth. Recognizing the link between fatigue, anxiety, and oral candidiasis can aid clinicians in evaluating patients holistically, prompting assessment for both psychological distress and possible infectious contributors when fatigue is reported.
